You can define a CDS custom entity with outbound call to the remote service and then consume the custom entity as a value help in your CDS modeling.

Regards,
Peter

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud SM: Mass Upload of Process Instances
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community members! 

I'm excited to share the improvements made to the Process Management screen in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). To improve efficiency when working with complex processes that contain a large number of similar activities, a new functionality has been introduced: Mass Upload of Process Settings. This feature enables users to download the configuration of existing process instances in the form of an XLSX file, make changes in required sheets, and then upload those changes back into the application to create multiple new instances simultaneously. 

Two new buttons have been added to the top toolbar – Download Process Settings and Upload Process Settings. The first enables users to download the configuration of existing instances as a template for further use, while the second is used for uploading a prepared XLSX file with new process instances. 

Downloading Process Instances 

To download the configuration of an existing process instance, select one or more instances from the list and click the Download Process Settings button. If only one instance is selected, a single XLSX file containing its configuration will be downloaded. In case multiple instances are selected, a ZIP file containing one XLSX file per instance will be downloaded.  

The name of the downloaded file will include the environment ID, version, and process instance ID, for example, RPM_TS05_PROC07.  

Each XLSX file has the same structure and includes five sheets:  

  • Process - Contains general information about process instances, including the following columns: Process, Description, Process Type, Process State, and Start Date. 
  • Activities - Contains information about activities from selected process instances, including the following columns: Process, Activity, Description, Performer, Reviewer, Start Date, and End Date.
  • Parameters - Contains information about defined parameters with the following columns: Process, Field, and Value.
  • Selections - Contains information about the defined selection with the following columns: Process, Selection Field, Logical Operator, Operator Type, Selection Min. Value and Selection Max. Values. For selections that don't require a minimum and maximum value, the value under Selection Min. Value will be taken as the selection value during upload.
  • Help - Provides additional information that might be useful when configuring the other four tabs. This tab is not considered during the upload process. 

While the structure and column order are always the same, the row content varies depending on which instance is selected – i.e., which activities, parameters, and selections it contains. 

Configuration of the XLSX file: 

  1. The XLSX file must maintain its structure exactly without changing the order of the columns, otherwise, the content cannot be placed correctly. 
  2. The Process State field on the Process sheet and the Logical Operator and Operator Type fields on the Selections sheet have dropdown menus, allowing you to easily select predefined available options. You can find more information about these values on the Help sheet. 
  3. The uploaded file should only contain activity, parameters and selections that exist and are active in the target process instance. If an XLSX file contains some elements that are not defined in the target process template, the upload process will be canceled. 
  4. If any elements (parameters, process selections, activities) that are part of the process template are not mentioned in the XLSX file, they will be copied from the target process instance. 
  5. If a field is defined in the Parameter or Selection sheet but has no value, it will be configured as an empty field. 
  6. Data is linked to the Process ID in the first column of each sheet. Rows with the same Process ID will be used to create a single process instance, and subsequent rows will be used accordingly. 
  7. The maximum number of process instances that can be uploaded within a single XLSX file is 50. If you exceed this limit, the system will automatically cancel the upload and display an appropriate error message. 

Uploading Process Instances 

Once you have finished configuring new instances in the XLSX file, the next step is to upload the file back into the PaPM application. On the Process Management screen, you first need to select one base instance, either the one for which you downloaded process settings or another instance with the same configuration, meaning using the same process template, so they contain the same Activity, Parameters and Selection fields. Clicking the Upload Process Settings button opens a file upload dialog, where you select the XLSX file that was configured in the previous steps. After confirming, the application will create new instances based on the data from all four tabs. 

It is important to note that the upload will not be successful if there is any error in the data, even if just one instance in the file is invalid, the entire upload process will be canceled, and no instances will be created. In such cases, a red toast message will appear, notifying you of the failure. For more details about the error, refer to the Message Log. 

Note: For the value field, no data validation will occur during the upload process, meaning the value field will not be checked if the values satisfy the defined configuration for that field. The process instance will be uploaded successfully without displaying any warning messages. This type of error can only be seen after uploading and expanding specific process instances, parameters, or selections, depending on where the error is present.   

The new Mass Upload of Process Settings functionality significantly speeds up the configuration of multiple similar processes, reduces manual work, and enables better standardization in the SAP PaPM Cloud SM environment. 

I hope you find these updates helpful!  

Stay tuned for the latest updates and insights in the next one. Thank you!

entry.title: Assistance Required: Issues with SCI Check Process and Virus Scan Warning in SAP
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am new to performing SCI checks in the SAP system and am currently facing an issue. Could someone please guide me through the process of performing SCI checks?

While executing the SCI check, I encountered a warning message: "Cannot Perform inspection: Virus scanner cannot be instantiated: Virus scan profile /SMIM_API/Put is not active." Since it is a warning, I proceeded further, but the results showed that no objects were processed, displaying zero. I have attached a snapshot of the result for your reference.

Could anyone advise on how to resolve this warning and ensure the SCI check processes the objects correctly? Any help would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you,
khaza Mohammad

entry.title: Migration story of 48 SAP SuccessFactors HCM successful integrations in 4 Months
entry.content:
1. Purpose of the document:

This document details how we migrated 48 SAP SuccessFactors HCM Boomi integrations to SAP Integration Suite successfully in 4 Months. We explain the strategy, execution, and different types of integration migration. 

2. Why SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer needs to migrate from Boomi to SAP Integration Suite: 

  1. Between 2013 and 2016, SAP SuccessFactors HCM customers used BOOMI as an integration platform to support data integration requirements in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation, and many customers are still running on Boomi
  2. SAP Customers have significantly invested in SAP BTP and SAP Integration Suite in the last seven years as part of S4HANA transitions and Integration Modernization (moving from SAP PO to SAP IS). 
  3. Customers are migrating interfaces from Boomi to the SAP Integration Suite, aligning with the SAP Integration strategy.
  4. Maintaining multiple integration platforms adds a lot of Technical Debt, and SAP customers consolidate and move to one integration platform (SAP Integration Suite) 

3. SAP SuccessFactors- Boomi Integration Landscape:

The customer implemented SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central in 2015 and utilized the BOOMI Integration Platform, building 48 integrations to support all Integration requirements.

 

 

4. Boomi to SAP Integration Suite Migration –  Framework:

Created a detailed migration framework to cover end to end execution 

 

 

Interface Mapping : Existing BOOMI interfaces evaluated  and mapped

 

 

5. Interface Development Approach Decision Process and Guidelines: 

 

 

  1. We implement prepackaged integrations developed by SAP to reduce development effort and customization. 
  2. We use SAP SuccessFactors Integration Center (IC) Templates for all simple to moderately complex Integrations.  
  3. We have built reusable assets to reuse . 
  4. Have used SF OData APIs. 
  5. Have checked with vendors about API integration capabilities to avoid the SFTP files approach. 
  6. Followed Real Time - Intelligent Services Based Event Driven approach wherever possible. 
  7. Strong governance in place and followed clean core capabilities and leveraged SAP IS standard capabilities. 
  8. Built reusable assets, and every consultant followed the same Integration standards. 
  9. Documented detailed technical specification document, created an integration design process and mapping document.

6. SFEC to SAP ERP Integrations Migration :

The customer was using custom Boomi integrations to replicate Emp and Org data, and manual file imports to update bank master data and cost center data.

  1. Implemented prepackaged integrations developed by SAP to integrate SAP SuccessFactors EC to SAP ERP – Employee and Org data
  2. Implemented Cost Center Prepackaged Integration to replicate Cost Center data from SAP ERP to SF EC
  3. Implemented Prepackaged Integration Template to replicate Bank Master Data from SAP ERP to SF EC

 

 

7. Instance Strategy and Interface Code Management :

 The customer had 3 BOOMI atoms, and we recommended that the customer get the 3 Integration Suite tenants and used the same.

  1. Have used SAP Cloud Transport Management to transport the code and implemented the governance process to make sure the the Integration Code will not transport without approval from the the business owner 

 

 

2. Cloud to On-Premise Connectivity

  • Customer did not use Boomi Local Atoms  - Hence, we didn’t go with Edge integration cell but used SAP Cloud Connector for SAP ERP integrations
  • Established connectivity with SFTP servers and used SSH connectivity option, and implemented Encryption
  • SAP IS IP ranges info shared with Vendors,, and they have whitelisted the same to have a a successful handshake

 

 

3. SAP SuccessFactors HCM - SAP Integration Suitelandscape after Migration

 

 

  1. Workforce Software Integrations migration – Implemented new prepackaged integration delivered by Workforce Software to replicate employee data from SF EC to WFS and used SAP Prepackaged integration to replicate Time and Absences data to EC Payroll
  2. ServiceNow Integrations – Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee and Contingents data using SAP IS – APIs approach
  3. Tableau Integration – Tableau is directly integrated with SF EC and imported employee and Org data – Direct Integration
  4. OC Tanner Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to OC Tanner
  5. ComputerShare Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to Computer Share
  6. SailPoint Integrations – SailPoint built a direct integration to integrate SAP SuccessFactors EC – SailPoint, and used the same replicate Employee, Org, and Foundation data and matched the same file layout as Boomi
  7. Qualtrics Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to Qualtrics
  8. Data Hub – Explored option to integrate Data Hub with SF EC directly APIs, but significant design changes and development required at Data Hub, hence built five master integrations to replicate Employee, Org, and Foundation data and matched the same file layout as Boomi
  9. Internal Systems Integrations – Generate the same file layouts as BOOMI but used  integration templates 

 8.  Key Considerations: 

 

 

  1. Engaging an SAP Integration Suite expert who is familiar with the Boomi integration platform is key.
  2. Migrating interfaces in a phased manner is a good idea
  3. Use this migration as an opportunity to reduce the tech debt and implement prepackaged integrations or Direct Integrations
  4. Creating good documentation is key, and delivering the requirement, technical design, and data mapping document is mandatory for each integration
  5. Make sure every part of the project follows SAP Integration Development standards and SF APIs leading practices
  6. Plan enough time to perform testing
  7. Build an internal team that can validate SI’s work
  8. Reduce the impact on vendors and make sure Boomi and SAP IS generate the same files

entry.title: Gamechanger Alert: Meet SAP Joule Studio for Real-World Impact
entry.content:
As someone who's still fairly new to SAP and learning about all it has to offer, I recently came across SAP Joule Studio—a tool that honestly surprised me. It’s part of SAP’s growing focus on AI, but instead of being just a tech buzzword, it’s something practical. Something helpful. Something that actually makes life easier.

So I thought I’d share what I’ve learned so far, for anyone else who’s curious like me.

 

What is SAP Joule Studio?

SAP Joule Studio is a low-code/no-code platform that lets you create AI-powered agents—little digital helpers that live inside your SAP system and support everyday tasks. These agents can:

  • Answer questions using real SAP data
  • Send reminders or alerts
  • Trigger workflows based on events
  • Help users navigate complex processes

The best part? You don’t need to be a developer to build one.

All you need is a good understanding of a business problem, and Joule Studio helps you turn that into a solution using easy visual tools.

 

Why This Is a Game-Changer (With Real Use Cases)

What really got me excited was seeing how real-world teams can use this tool to solve day-to-day challenges. Here are a few examples that really stood out to me:

✅HR & Employee Self-Service

  • Use Case: An AI agent that answers common HR questions like “How many vacation days do I have left?”, “What’s the process to update my bank details?”, or “When does my probation end?”
  • Impact: Reduces emails to HR, gives instant answers, and improves employee satisfaction.

✅Onboarding Assistant

  • Use Case: An AI agent that guides a new joiner through their first week—showing them how to complete paperwork, introducing them to teammates, and sending daily checklists.
  • Impact: Makes onboarding smoother, especially for remote hires or global teams.

✅Procurement Alerts

  • Use Case: A smart assistant that watches for delays from key suppliers and notifies procurement managers if a PO is stuck or overdue.
  • Impact: Keeps procurement responsive and improves relationships with vendors.

✅Project Management Summary

  • Use Case: A daily AI briefing that tells project leads about overdue tasks, timesheet gaps, or resource bottlenecks.
  • Impact: Saves hours of manual tracking and keeps teams aligned.

✅Compliance Checker

  • Use Case: A bot that checks which employees haven't completed their mandatory training or signed policy documents, and sends them a reminder.
  • Impact: Helps HR stay compliant without manual follow-ups.

✅IT Helpdesk Agent

  • Use Case: An agent that answers questions like “How do I reset my SAP password?” or “Where do I raise a ticket?”
  • Impact: Cuts down ticket volume for IT support and empowers users.

These aren’t just theoretical—they’re real issues we see in companies every day. And Joule Studio gives you a way to solve them quickly, without coding or waiting on IT.

 

Why Clients Will Love It Too

For clients, Joule Studio brings:

  • Self-service power to their SAP users
  • Less dependency on IT for small tasks
  • Smarter workflows with reminders, alerts, and guidance
  • Faster onboarding and training for new team members
  • Consistency across geographies and business units

AI becomes something helpful, not something scary or complex. It empowers users instead of overwhelming them.

 

Where You Can See It in Action

If you're like me and prefer learning visually, I highly recommend this official demo:

 SAP Joule Studio – AI for Everyone (YouTube)

You can also explore SAP’s official product page for more examples and how-to guides:
 SAP Joule Studio – Official Page

These are great starting points with visuals and real examples.

 

Getting Started with Joule Studio

If you’re ready to try it:

  1. Make sure your system is running on SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform).
  2. Go into SAP Build and access Joule Studio.
  3. Start by creating a small AI agent for a common task.
  4. Train it using real process examples and test in Preview mode.
  5. Roll it out with the right user permissions.

 

 

 

Remember—you don’t need to be technical. You just need to understand your business and what people are struggling with. Joule Studio helps you close that gap.

 

Final Thoughts (From One Learner to Another)

I’m still exploring SAP, still learning, and still asking questions. But tools like Joule Studio give me hope, not just for where tech is going, but for how we can make it work for people.

This isn’t about replacing humans—it’s about supporting them with small, smart helpers that remove friction and free up time.

If you're also exploring SAP tools and wondering where to start with AI, this might just be the right place. Try building a simple use case and see what it unlocks for your team.

And if you’ve tried it, I’d really love to hear what you’ve built. Let’s share and learn together, because the best ideas often come from the people closest to the problem.

 

#SAPJoule, #JouleStudio, #SAPBTP, #AIinSAP, #Gamechanger, #NoCodeAI, #SAPBuild, #EnterpriseAI, #DigitalTransformation, #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Re: ABAP RAP: Upload data from Excel file
entry.content:
1. Yes, a extension i necessary. The documentation can be found under GettingStarted with samples for Fiori Elements and Freestyle.
But no, not the binary is send to the backend but the data.
The component extracts the data from the excel, parses and validates it and send then as data to the backend. Thats why you can use any OData Backend.
2. No need to create a action, the standard Create and Update process is used.

So no need to handle binary data in the backend.

entry.title: Seamless Integration: Consuming SAP API Hub APIs in On-Premise SAP Applications
entry.content:
Have you ever got a client requirement to use SAP API hub API in SAP object and struggled to find a correct approach. It happened with me as well. Therefore, I decided to share step by step instructions to handle it with all of you.

 

Add Product Master API in SAP Gateway

 

  1. Register Product Master API in SAP GATEWAY

 

2. Enter the System Alias and External Service Name and click Get Services.

3. Select the service and Click Add Selected Services.

4. Enter package details and continue.

5. Filter gateway with added services.

6. Click SAP Gateway Client to test the service.

7. Click Execute.

Utilize this Odata service in Standard Badi

  1. Create implementation for Badi BADI_MATERIAL_CHECK

Go to SE19 >> Enter BADI name

Click Display

2. Go to Implementation >> Create

3. Provide implementation name and continue.

Add short text and Click Save

Add package details

4. Implement method

Go to Interface >> Check Data

 METHOD if_ex_badi_material_check~check_data.
**********************************************
*   Purpose: Call Product Master API         *
*--------------------------------------------*
*   Author : Sangeeta Singh                  *
**********************************************

    DATA: lv_matnr         TYPE matnr,
          lo_http_client   TYPE REF TO if_http_client,
          lv_request_url   TYPE string,
          lv_response_data TYPE string,
          lv_url           TYPE string.

    CONSTANTS: lc_url TYPE string VALUE 'https://<your_s4hana_host>:<port>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PRODUCT_SRV/'.
    " **CRITICAL: Replace with your actual service URL**

    "Get material number
    lv_matnr = wmara-matnr.
    lv_url = lc_url.

    "Run API call if material found in above step
    IF lv_matnr IS NOT INITIAL.
      CALL METHOD cl_http_client=>create_by_url
        EXPORTING
          url    = lv_url
        IMPORTING
          client = lo_http_client
        EXCEPTIONS
          OTHERS = 4.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        lo_http_client->request->set_method( 'GET' ).
        lo_http_client->send( ).
        lo_http_client->receive( ).
        lv_response_data = lo_http_client->response->get_cdata( ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

5. Save and Activate.

Test API

  1. Go to T-Code MM02

Enter any available material

2. Select Basic data

3. Change description and click Save.

Obviously, it is a very basic implementation, much more can be done. However, it is good to start with.

Thanks for vising!

Keep learning!!

Sangeeta Singh

Website

Linkedin
Medium
ABAP Development   SAP Community  API API Management

entry.title: How to Connect a Third-Party Application to SAP CPI Using APIs and Access Token – A GreytHR Example
entry.content:
👉   Introduction
In modern integration scenarios, many third-party applications expose APIs to exchange data. SAP
Integration Suite (CPI) allows us to consume these APIs easily, even when token-based authentication is
required.
In this blog, I’ll walk you through how I integrated the GreytHR HRMS application with SAP using API
endpoints and access tokens. We'll fetch employee master data using a secure, token-based flow.

👉Topics Covered
• What is an API First Approach?
• What are APIs?
• Types of APIs.
• using API How to connect third party Applications.

•  What is an API First Approach?
The API First Approach is an approach to software design that focuses on the API to create applications that can be easily connected to each other. API First creates ecosystems of applications that are modular, reusable, and extensible, just like Lego bricks.


An API First Approach means that your APIs are treated as first class citizens. Everything revolves around the end product being used by mobile devices and client applications. An API First approach involves developing APIs that are consistent and reusable. This is achieved by using an API description language.

                                       

The graphic shows an example of API First Approach. Information is provided in the following text.
Explanation:


API Provider :
An API provider provides an interface with technical features (No. 1). Symbolically, this is shown in this picture with the ball- or lollipop notation. This interface can be consumed by a service (No. 5). There presentation corresponds to the socket notation (No. 2). The service acts in the role of the API consumer.

API Consumer :
The API consumer requires an interface (No. 3). The representation corresponds to the socket notation (No. 4). In this case, the API provider is the service (No. 5). The presentation is again made with the ball-or lollipop notation.

Find More Information at:
Understanding the API-First Approach to Building Products.
It is not Cloud first or API first but Strategy first. API Management Strategy in Mult iCloud Environments | SAP Blogs.

• What are APIs?
API stands for Application Programming Interface. An API specifies the operations as well as inputs and outputs of a software component. It defines functionalities that are independent of their respective implementations, so that these implementations can vary without affecting the user of the API.


• Types of APIs.
In the literature, there are many overviews of different types of APIs. Below is a simple overview of the APIs that we must understand for this course.

The graphic shows an overview of APIs. Information is provided in the following text.
Here, four different APIs are defined under the superset of APIs, based on their use.
Databased APIs These are intended for file exchange between systems. Files can be, for example, configuration files.


Object-orientated APIs
These are used in object-oriented programming languages to define the communication of the classes with each other.
Remote APIs (No. 1)
This includes today's important web APIs, such as REST and SOAP APIs. REST itself is not a protocol but a software architectural style.
Messaging APIs (No. 2)
•  These are asynchronous APIs that send events based on events. These are used in Event Driven
architectures.


• using API How to connect Third party Applications.
I am trying to connect the GreytHR application to SAP CPI using APIs.
Step-1
✅ Step 1: Create API in GreytHR
• Log into your GreytHR portal
• Navigate to the API Management section
• Click Create API
• Provide:

• API Name
• Role access (select “All Roles”)

Click on Next Save the generated Username and Password

✅ Step 2: Store Credentials in CPI
• In SAP Integration Suite, go to:
• Monitoring → Manage Security → Security Material
• Create a User Credential
• Enter the username and password received from GREYTHR
• Name it: GREYTHR_CREDENTIALS

✅ Step 3: Create Access Token Flow in CPI
• Open your design workspace and create a new Integration Flow
• Use a Timer as the start event (for scheduled execution)
• Add a Request-Reply block
• Use an HTTP Adapter with:
• Method: POST
• URL: GreytHR’s Access Token Endpoint
• Authentication: use GREYTHR_CREDENTIALS

✅ Step 4: Convert JSON to XML (if necessary) and Store Access Token
If the response is in JSON format (which is common for most token APIs):
• Add a JSON to XML Converter immediately after the Request-Reply step
• This ensures the token response can be processed in SAP CPI (which primarily works with XML)
If the response is already in XML format, you can skip the converter step
• Add a Content Modifier after the token call
• Extract the ACCESS_TOKEN from the response JSON
• Store it as a property: ${header . ACCESS_TOKEN}

✅ Step 5: Call GreytHR API to Fetch Data
• Add another Request-Reply block
• HTTP Adapter configuration:
• Method: GET or POST
• URL: GREYTHR API endpoint for employee data
• Authorization: Bearer ${header. ACCESS_TOKEN}

Deploy the iFlow
• If successful, you’ll receive employee master data from GREYTHR!

 👉 Conclusion
• Using APIs and token-based authentication, SAP CPI can securely connect to third-party systems
like GreytHR. This approach is:
• Scalable
• Reusable
• Suitable for any modern integration scenario
The same logic can be extended to tools like Salesforce, Zoho, or OpenWeatherMap.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud - Best Practices
entry.content:
Please note that this article is part of a Dashboard Design Best-Practices blog series,
created to assist you in designing and building meaningful dashboards with SAP Analytics Cloud.
Click here to open the Dashboard Design Best-Practices blog series hub page in a new window

The focus in this blog is about Best-Practices when building  analytical applications in SAP Analytics Cloud.
If you don't have the template yet, you'll find the necessary information on how to get it in this blog post.

 -> The available links always open in a new browser tab.

 

Best-Practices

  1. Template Layout Types
  2. Layout Settings in SAC
  3. How are the SAC components nested in the Outline structure? 
  4. How users can indicate the Linked Analysis relation on a page?  
  5. Theme CSS Options
  6. Add Spacing between widgets in Logic Groups 
  7. Hide Icon on Input controls 
  8. Activate "Group Data"- Function for widgets
  9. Set "View Time" Settings 
  10. Performance Consoderations

 

1. Template Layout Types

With the new version of the SAC Fiori template, 4 pages within the template are now available.
The templates differ mainly in their layout and two different header types: Floating and Sticky Header

Floating Header (Template 1/ 2/ 4)
The floating header disappears when scrolling down, has rounded corners and a spacing of 16px to the edges or nearby layout components. It is a space-saving solution if certain information must not always be visible. 
Sticky Header  (Template 3)

The sticky header stays always visible at the top, even when the user scrolls through the content.
The header has no rounded corners and no padding to the left, right and top.
Use the subtitle section to show information that relates to the whole page. 
E.g. instructions, explanatory text, meaning of abbreviations, time periods, special conditions, etc.

The 2 headers can be used for all kind of scenarios like Start/ Home/ Sub/ Detail Pages

 

Template 1:

This template contains all the different combinations of widgets. (See also Dashboard Design Patterns Blog)
Starting with the Info Group and Control Group on Top and different characteristics of Logic Groups and single widgets with or without Input Controls. You can quickly copy and paste them or simply delete if you don't need them. 

All-in-one Template with floating header

Template 2:

Unlike Template 1, the Info Group is positioned vertically.  The info group, also called KPI row, usually provides an high-level overview on the most important numbers at a glance. Depending on the use case, this positioning may be a better alternative.

Template 3:

Template 3 differs from the others by the use of a "sticky header" and a "Collapse/ Expand Logic Group" Function.

 

Template 4:

Template 4 is intended for so-called exploration scenarios. A filter row and two chart types offer a quick way to to drill down
information using the Linked Analysis functionality in SAC and then display the details of the selection in a table.

 

2. Layout Settings in SAC

When creating a dashboard, you have a practical way to control the layout behavior of your app elements.
Use the device selection at the bottom of the SAC Canvas to simulate the possible screen size.

Make sure that the content is displayed appropriately on the end devices that the end user will be using.
The content should work visually up to a resolution of 1920x1080.

 

3. How are the SAC components nested in the Outline structure?

Element Order in Outline
While the contents of the individual sections, which are on one level, are arranged from top to bottom, the presentation in the outline is reversed. Therefore, what is at the top visually is at the bottom in the outline structure.

 

Visualized Page Structure

The next image shows the individual patterns of Template_1.
You can find more information in the Layout Patterns Blog Article.

To better understand the template structure, the structural elements as well as the relevant components of the Template_1 and their usage in the SAC outline are shown in the next picture:

  

Fiori Card Design
To achieve the Fiori card design on e.g. a panel, the CSS class name “card” is used in the styling panel of the respective panel. 

 

4. How users can indicate the Linked Analysis relation on a page?

Linked Analysis gives the user the ability to define, which other chart widgets in the analysis are affected when applying selections while browsing hierarchical data. However, to give the user a clear understanding of which widgets on the page will be affected, two scenarios are possible.

Scenario 1: 
Linked Analysis on complete page content
If a data point selection in one widget affects all other widgets on the page, it is possible to use panels for each individual widget. Since the selection affects the entire page, it is not necessary to group visually.

 

Scenario 2: 
Linked Analysis on parts of the page
If a data point selection in a widget only affects some widgets on the page, this should be visually indicated to the user. For this purpose, a (Logical Group) Panel should be used as a visual bracket of the related and influenced content

 

 

5. Theme CSS Options

When using the Fiori Morning Horizon theme, CSS classes are used from the beginning.
The Global Default Class Name "saptheme" is set automatically.
For further customization options at the component level, use the CSS class name for the corresponding component which can be found in the following table.

 

TitleWidget TypeCSS Class Name
Page TitleTextpage__title
Page SubtitleTextpage__subtitle
Page Description TitleTextpage__desc__title
Page DescriptionTextpage__desc__content
LinkTextlink
Negative TextTexttext--negative
Critical TextTexttext--critical
Positive TextTexttext--positive
Informative TextTexttext--information
Neutral TextTexttext--neutral
Fiori CardFlow Layout Panel, Panelcard
Emphasized ButtonButtonbutton--emphasized
   
   
   

 

6. Add Spacing between widgets in Logic Groups 

There is currently no option to set a padding between individual widgets.
Therefore, it can happen that widgets within a logical group are visually very close to each other.

 To avoid this, there is a workaround by adding invisible borders to the widget.

Step 1 
Select the first widget, open the styling panel in the right panel and select “Right Border”, no color value and a Line Width of 8px.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Step 2
Select the second widget in the middle, add “Left and Right Borders", no color value and a Line Width of 8px.

 

Step 3
Select the third widget, add “Left Border", no color value and a Line Width of 8px.

Result
The result is a clearer separation of the different widgets.

 

7. Hide Icon on Input controls 

Initial State
SAC first displays an additional icon when a filter or input control is created. 
For reasons of simplicity, we recommend hiding this icon. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

How-To
Open the context menu of the filter widget, navigate to the header icon ("Show/Hide -Group)
and deselect the checkmark.

 

 

 

 

Result

 

 

 

 

8. Activate "Group Data"- Function for widgets

9. Set "View Time" Settings

We recommend to use the "Present Mode" as default mode for analytical pages

1. Open Side Panel by selecting the "Story Settings" icon and the corresponding sub-items in the SAC toolbar.

 

 

 

 

 

2. Select “Present Mode” in the Navigation Panel and click the Done Button.
-> Do not forget to save the story.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

10. Performance Considerations

 

Performance of the dashboard has a big impact on the users’ experience. Poor performance results in poor user experience. When designing and building the dashboard, consider the factors that affect the performance. In general, the performance gets poorer:

  • with more data,
  • with more complexity,
  • with more data that needs to be loaded simultaneously.

Considering performance in your design will result in trade-offs. For instance, linked analysis and filters will impact the performance, yet are very useful and powerful features. You’ll need to evaluate how big the performance impact is and whether your users will be more appreciative of the power than annoyed at the wait. 

 

Reduce the amount of data used

  • If specific data is not required in the dashboard, consider
    not showing it and then don’t include the data in the model. 

 

Do aggregations early, before calculations

  • Aggregating data before it is loaded into the SAC model results in many fewer rows in the model,
    which means SAC will require less computing time to process those rows when creating the charts. 

 

Use the most performant model for your requirements

  • Typically, an acquired data model is faster than live connections.
    If you don’t need live data, consider an acquired data model. 
  • Typically, an analytical model is faster than a planning model.
    If you don’t need a planning model, consider an analytical model. 

 

Reduce the number of expensive elements that are shown together

  • Reduce the number of charts on one page. 
  • Reduce the number of high-resolution images. 
  • Use trellis charts rather than individual yet similar charts. 
  • Consider where to show geo-charts and scatterplots with many points
    as they are less performant than bar charts. 

 

Reduce the number of elements that require processing

  • Consider how many filters and what type of filters are needed, as they can impact performance. 
  • Reduce the number of widgets that are linked by the linked analysis feature,
    as that can impact the performance.

entry.title: Timer and Trigger based SAP CPI Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, we’ll explore how to design a single CPI interface that will work with scheduled based and also on demand trigger based.

Business Scenario

In most SuccessFactors integrations, we often need to schedule interfaces based on a timer. These interfaces read messages from SuccessFactors using the last modified date timestamp and process the data to target applications. Once the data is sent to the target application, we update the last modified date and store it in a variable. During the next run, the interface reads data based on the timestamp and processes the records.

We design the interface to avoid updating the timestamp if any errors occur. For example:

  • If the target application is down or there are connection issues.
  • If mandatory data is missing for some cases.

This approach works well if the target application processes all records as a single message. However, there are situations where we need to read all employee data during a specific time interval and process each employee's data individually employee by employee. In the Splitter:

  • If we enable stop on exception it will halt all record processing.
  • If we do not enable stop on exception we might miss processing some records.

For example, if we apply a last modified date query to employment data and encounter an issue, we can correct it, and the next run will process the modified data.

In another case, if we navigate to emergency contact data from the employment portlet and apply the last modified date query on employment data, any error in the emergency contact data will prevent the record from being processed to the target. Even after updating the emergency contact, it will not process because the query's last modified date is based on employment and not on emergency contact. To handle this, we can create another integration process with a trigger-based approach in the same iFlow to query and retrieve the data, then process it from the local integration. With this trigger we can manually run the integration by passing the employee id.

Solution Architecture

 

 

SAP CPI Development 

 

In the above SAP CPI screenshot we have two integration Process

  1. Time Based Integration: This will execute based on the pre-defined time interval Steps are below explained
    • Content Modifier for Schedule Query: This one will be used to read time stamp and also scheduled query to pass in next step.
    • Local Integration Process: This one will be main local integration process, based on the query received, it will call SF and do mapping and send details to the target application.
    • Write Time stamp in the variable. This will store last modified date to process in next run.
    • Application Id, this is optional , used for additional monitoring to know from and last modified date.
    • Below is Externalized Content Modifier

 

  1. Trigger Based Integration: This will have HTTP sender and it is trigger based. An Ad-Hoc basic, team will call this end point. We can use this in some UI and give it to business to run themselves
    • Content Modifier for Trigger Query: This one will be used read parameters from trigger and form the query to next step.
    • Local Integration Process: This one will be main local integration process, based on the query received, it will call SF and do mapping and send details to the target application.
    • Additional logging.
    • Below is externalized query in Content Modifier 

 

 

Once iflow deployed it will looks like below

  • Trigger endpoint URL
  • Timer execution for schedule.

 

 

Testing

Schedule Run

In the Timer I have used Application message id to know the time interval.

Trigger Run

postman for trigger

Monitoring

 

Note in the Trigger content modifier I have used ${header.SapAuthenticatedUserName} to know who triggered iflow.

Conclusion

We can design SAP CPI ilfows to work with  both Timer based and also trigger based approaches. Depending on the requirements we can modify these as needed. Additionally,  we can provide a trigger endpoint to API or UI application for business team use.

SAP Integration Suite API Management SAP SuccessFactors Platform

entry.title: How to Use External Files in SAP Cloud ALM for Smarter Documentation
entry.content:
How to Use External Files in SAP Cloud ALM for Smarter Documentation

Introduction

In today’s fast-paced project environments, seamless document access and collaboration are vital. SAP Cloud ALM's Documents app offers robust features to manage content—but one standout addition is the External File option. This feature bridges the gap between ALM processes and externally managed documents.

Purpose

The purpose of this blog is to:

  • Highlight the role of external file linking within SAP Cloud ALM.
  • Explain how this functionality supports flexibility and collaboration across teams.
  • Guide users in leveraging this feature to improve documentation efficiency.

Types of Documents You Can Add as External Files

You can link to any file type stored outside SAP Cloud ALM, such as:

  • Microsoft Word documents (.docx) – for functional specs, meeting notes, etc
  • Excel spreadsheets (.xlsx) – for test cases, data mapping, or tracking sheets.
  • PDF files – for signed approvals, training guides, or vendor documentation.
  • PowerPoint presentations (.pptx) – for project kick-offs, demos, or stakeholder updates.
  • Google Docs/Sheets/Slides – for real-time collaboration.
  • Links to SharePoint, OneDrive, or internal file servers – for centralized access to live documents.

Here is how it works:

  • If you upload an external file directly through the Documents app, it gets stored in a dedicated repository within SAP BTP DMS.
  • If you simply link to a file hosted elsewhere (like SharePoint or Google Drive), then the file remains in that external location—SAP Cloud ALM just references it via URL.

Request the Required Services

In your SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) subaccount:

  • Subscribe to SAP Document Management Service, Integration Option
  • Subscribe to SAP Document Management Service, Repository Option

These are available in the SAP BTP service marketplace.

When you subscribe to the Repository Option and Integration Option, SAP Cloud ALM will automatically create and bind a dedicated internal repository the first time you access the Documents app.

 

This repository is the only one supported for Cloud ALM—external or manually created repositories are not allowed.

 

No manual onboarding via API is needed for this specific use case.

Prerequisites

Before using the External File feature, ensure the following.

  • You have access to SAP Cloud ALM with the Documents app enabled.
  • External file repositories (e.g., SharePoint, Google Drive, internal servers) are accessible to your project team.
  • Appropriate permission and sharing settings are configured in your external storage systems.
  • Establish connection between SAP Document management service and Cloud ALM tenant.

External file configuration steps:

Step1: Go to implementation tab in cloud ALM.

 

 

Step2: Click on Document tile.

 

 

Step3: Click on create in the Document screen.

 

 

Step4: Click on external file and follow the configuration process

 

 

For external file below setup are required:

 

Procedure

  1. Navigate to the space that you created in Creating a Subaccount and a Space.
  2. In the left pane, choose Services Service Marketplace.

 

  1. Find the Document Management Service, Integration Option tile in the list of services, and choose the tile to open the overview page.

 

  1. Choose More Options and select Create.

 

  1. In the left pane, choose Instances and Subscriptions to access the instance of Document Management Service, Integration Option. For more information about creating an instance, see Creating Service Instances.
  2. After creating the Document Management Service generate the service key.

 

 

 

 

 

Follow the below steps for creating destination in BTP account.

 

 There is template available in Sap help portal document you can download and import in BTP destination.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After the DMS destination connection setup, come back to cloud ALM documentation app and check the external file tab. If the setup done correctly, you could see the upload option in the external file tab.

In this option you can upload the external file, here you can upload the document.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Click on browse and add the document. Choose Upload to store a file in the SAP BTP DMS repository. The file name and size will appear in the External File tab. A draft version of the document is automatically created.

If the draft is discarded, the file is also deleted. To finalize, select Create to save the document. For more information-https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/external-files

 

 

 

If you want to replace your external file with latest version, then you must go on edit mode add the latest version of file which you have selected for replacement.

 

For deletion of any external file, you must go on edit mode, select the external file, and delete. As a result, only the file in the SAP BTP DMS repository is removed, while the document remains. Deleting a document with a file: You can delete documents in the list view. If an external file is attached, it is deleted as well.

 

Has external file you can enable in Document list view.

 

 

 

In Additional information tab add details: Context, status and planning etc.

 

 

Note

  • Per document, you can add only one external file.
  • The maximum size per file is 10 MB.
  • When you upload an external file, draft handling is applied in the same way as with the main document during its creation.
  • You can decide to only work on the external file or maintain content in the content tab.

 

If you are getting any issue while configuration please check:

  • Ensure your entitlements are correctly assigned in the BTP cockpit.
  • Make sure the trust configuration between your BTP subaccount and Cloud ALM tenant is in place.
  • If you are using custom roles or access policies, you may need to assign permissions for users to upload and manage documents.

 

Conclusion: The External File feature is a small but powerful addition to SAP Cloud ALM. It respects the reality of hybrid work environments and empowers teams to stay agile, connected, and efficient.

 

For information refer this link

 Connecting SAP Document Management Service | SAP Help Portal

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/creating-documents

entry.title: KKA2 Result analysis Planned cost Vs Actual Cost
entry.content:
Dear Expert,

We are working in a construction industry scenario where projects typically span over extended durations. In such cases, project costs are incurred and booked in the current month, while customer billing is usually submitted in the following month. Therefore, the system is expected to calculate and post accrued income (WIP) for the work completed within the current period.

In our case:

  • A sales order is mapped to the WBS element. Sales order value = 12,000

  • Budget cost and cost planning against the WBS is 10000

  • Actual cost posted = 1,500

However, when we execute Result Analysis (T-Code: KKA2), the system shows the planned cost as equal to the actual cost (1,500) instead of reflecting the 10,000 planned value.

Could you please guide us on whether there are any configuration settings or planning methods we need to review to ensure the planned cost is correctly reflected during result analysis?

Appreciate your support in resolving this issue.

 

Thanks & Regards,

silambu

entry.title: Re: PO Output form gets overwritten when PO is changed
entry.content:
Hi Wasel, 

Are you on S4 or on ECC? Was the form already sent or was it still pending? Normally the PO form will always show the latest data, because it is dynamically generated by the forms program when you call it up. The best option is to make sure that the output is written to a storage after output. In ECC that can easily be done by setting the "Print and archive" checkmark in the output type. In S4 this should be possible as well, but I don't know the settings. 

 

Kind regards, Sjaak

entry.title: Custom program to generate- Where used list for CDS DDLS Object.
entry.content:
This code is not my original creation, there was already a utility code on SCN to generate the where used list of CDS DDLS object. But that code only works for 1 CDS at a time. I supplied the same code to my team and they said it would be better if it could handle multiple entries. So, I just made those few changes rest is as it is from the original and full credit goes to that developer for his/her work. This code can surely be improved using better queries and reducing loops etc. but that will be up to the developer who uses this code in future.

 

report zpsb_util_cds_where_used.


types:begin of ty_ddls_w,
        ddls     type ddlname,
        o_type   type trobjtype,
        obj_name type progname,
      end of ty_ddls_w,

      begin of ty_funcm,
        funcname type rs38l_fnam,
        pname    type pname,
      end of ty_funcm,

      ty_ddls_t  type standard table of ty_ddls_w with empty key,
      ty_funcm_t type standard table of ty_funcm with empty key.

data:w_ddls_f  type string,
     t_ddls_w  type ty_ddls_t,
     t_ddls_final  type ty_ddls_t,
     s_ddlname type ty_ddls_w,
     t_func    type ty_funcm_t.

selection-screen: begin of block b1 with frame title text-001.

selection-screen skip 1.

selection-screen begin of line.
*parameters:p_rad1 radiobutton group rb1.
selection-screen comment 5(20) comment1.
"PARAMETERS: p_ddls1  TYPE ddlname.
select-options: s_ddls1 for s_ddlname-ddls NO INTERVALS.
selection-screen end of line.

selection-screen skip 1.

*selection-screen begin of line.
*parameters:p_rad2 radiobutton group rb1.
*selection-screen comment 5(20) comment2.
*parameters: p_ddls2  type ddlname.
*selection-screen end of line.
selection-screen:end of block b1.

at selection-screen output.

  comment1 = 'CDS Where Used:'.
*  comment2 = 'CDS Components:'.

class lcl_event_handler definition.

  public section.

    types:begin of lty_source_code,
            src_cd type char1000_idoc,
          end of lty_source_code.

    data:
      lo_alv type ref to cl_salv_table,
      lt_alv type ty_ddls_t.


    methods constructor
      importing
        i_alv type ref to cl_salv_table.

    methods on_double_click
      for event if_salv_events_actions_table~double_click
                  of cl_salv_events_table
      importing row column.

endclass.

class lcl_event_handler implementation.

  method constructor.
    lo_alv = i_alv.
  endmethod.

  method on_double_click.

    data: lt_source_cd type standard table of lty_source_code,
          l_obj_name   type progname,
          l_type       type trobjtype,
          l_ddls       type ddlname.

    check column = 'OBJ_NAME'.

    l_obj_name = lt_alv[ row ]-obj_name.
    l_type = lt_alv[ row ]-o_type.
    l_ddls = lt_alv[ row ]-ddls.

    case l_type.

      when 'DDLS'.

        try.
            cl_dd_ddl_handler_factory=>create( )->read(
                  exporting
                    name         = conv ddlname( to_upper( l_obj_name ) )
                  importing
                    ddddlsrcv_wa = data(ddlsrcv_wa) ).
          catch cx_dd_ddl_read into data(exc).
            cl_demo_output=>display( exc->get_text( ) ).
        endtry.
        if ddlsrcv_wa-source is initial.
          cl_demo_output=>display( |Nothing found| ).
          return.
        endif.

        cl_demo_output=>display( replace( val = ddlsrcv_wa-source
                                          sub = |\n|
                                          with = ``
                                          occ = 0 ) ).

      when others.

*---get the source code (other then DDLS)
        read report l_obj_name  into lt_source_cd.
        if sy-subrc eq 0.

          data(l_source_code) = reduce string( init lv_src type string
*---Don't consider ANY commented code
                                        for lw_source in lt_source_cd
                                    index into lv_index
                                      where ( table_line+0(1) ne '*' )
                              next lv_src=to_upper( lv_src ) &&
                              space && to_upper( lw_source-src_cd )  && | LINE#: { lv_index }#|   ).

          clear lt_source_cd.

          data(l_search_pattern) = `(?:LINE#: (\d)+)(?=#[\w\s]+` && |{  l_ddls  }| && `)`.
          data(lv_result) = match(  val = l_source_code
                                               regex = l_search_pattern
                                               case = abap_false    ).
          if lv_result is not initial.
            data(lv_line) = match(
                                     val = lv_result
                                    regex = `(?:LINE#:\s)([0-9]+)`
                                    case = abap_false
                                     ).
            split lv_line at ':' into data(l_part1) data(l_part2).
            condense l_part2 no-gaps.


          else.
            message |Object: { l_ddls }, has no direct usage| type 'S'.
          endif.

        endif.
        call function 'RS_TOOL_ACCESS'
          exporting
            operation           = 'SHOW'
            object_name         = l_obj_name
            object_type         = l_type
            position            = cond char10( when l_part2 = 0 then space else ( l_part2 + 1 ) )
          exceptions
            not_executed        = 1
            invalid_object_type = 2
            others              = 3.
        if sy-subrc <> 0.
* Implement suitable error handling here
        endif.
    endcase.
  endmethod.

endclass.

start-of-selection.

*  case abap_true.
*
*    when p_rad1.

*      IF p_ddls1 IS INITIAL.
      if s_ddls1[] is initial.
        message 'Enter a CDS View to proceed!' type 'S'.
        leave list-processing.
      endif.

      loop at s_ddls1 into data(p_ddls1).



        w_ddls_f = '\TY:' && p_ddls1-low.

*---Find parent DDLS (if any)
        select * from ddls_ris_index
        into table (t_ddls_where_used)
        where used_artifact_fullname = @w_ddls_f.
        if sy-subrc eq 0.
          loop at t_ddls_where_used reference into data(lo_ddls).
            append initial line to t_ddls_w reference into data(lo_ddls_wu).
            split lo_ddls->used_artifact_fullname at ':' into data(w_part1) data(w_part2).

            lo_ddls_wu->ddls = w_part2.
            lo_ddls_wu->o_type = 'DDLS'.
            lo_ddls_wu->obj_name = lo_ddls->ddlsrc_name.
          endloop.
        endif.

*----find all other ABAP Objects where input DDLS been used
        select name,
               include,
               case repo~subc

               when '1' then 'REPS'
               when 'K' then 'CLAS'
               when 'I' then 'INCL'
               when 'F' then 'FUGR'
               else 'UN'
               end  as o_type

            from wbcrossgt as wb
            left outer join reposrc as repo
            on wb~include = repo~progname
        INTO table (lt_wbcrossgt)
        where otype = 'TY'
        and name = @p_ddls1-low
        and indirect = .
        if sy-subrc eq 0.
          sort lt_wbcrossgt by name include o_type.

*---Check for Function Modules
          loop at lt_wbcrossgt reference into data(lo_wbcross) where o_type = 'UN'.
            append initial line to t_func reference into data(lo_func).
            lo_func->funcname = lo_wbcross->include.
          endloop.

          if t_func is not initial.
            select  funcname,
                    pname
              from tfdir INTO table (t_tfdir)
              for all entries in @t_func
              where funcname = @t_func-funcname.
            if sy-subrc eq 0.
              sort t_tfdir by funcname.
            endif.
          endif.

          loop at lt_wbcrossgt reference into lo_wbcross.
            append initial line to t_ddls_w reference into lo_ddls_wu.

            if lo_wbcross->o_type = 'UN' and line_exists( t_tfdir[ funcname = lo_wbcross->include ] ).
              lo_ddls_wu->ddls = p_ddls1.
              lo_ddls_wu->o_type = 'FUNC'.
              lo_ddls_wu->obj_name = lo_wbcross->include.
            else.

              lo_ddls_wu->ddls = p_ddls1.
              lo_ddls_wu->o_type = lo_wbcross->o_type.
              lo_ddls_wu->obj_name = lo_wbcross->include.
            endif.

          endloop.

        endif.
        append LINES OF t_ddls_w[] TO t_ddls_final[].
        clear: t_ddls_w, t_func, t_tfdir.
      endloop.

*      WHEN OTHERS.
*
**
**    when p_rad2.
**      message 'This feature, not yet Supported' type 'S'.
**      leave list-processing.
*  endcase.

*  if t_ddls_w is not initial.
  if t_ddls_final[] is not initial.

    call method cl_salv_table=>factory
      importing
        r_salv_table = data(lo_alv1)
      changing
        t_table      = t_ddls_final[].

    if lo_alv1 is bound.
      lo_alv1->get_functions( )->set_all( abap_true ).
      lo_alv1->get_columns( )->set_optimize( abap_true ).

      data(lo_evt_handler) = new lcl_event_handler( lo_alv1 ).
      data(lo_events) = lo_alv1->get_event( ).
      set handler lo_evt_handler->on_double_click for lo_events.

      lo_evt_handler->lt_alv = t_ddls_w.
      lo_alv1->display( ).
    endif.

*  else.
*
*    message 'No records found!' type 'S'.
*    leave list-processing.
*
  endif.

 

 

There is no line by line explanation or comment because the code is self-explanatory. Talk less code more 🙂

entry.title: Item Visibility in SF-LMS When User Language Setting Is UK/US and Item Language Is Default
entry.content:
1. Has anyone encountered visibility issues with learning items that are assigned a “Default” language setting, particularly when the user’s language preference is set to localized versions like English (UK) or English (US)?
  2. What is the system’s fallback behavior when the user’s preferred language doesn't match the exact language setting of the item? Example: Will items set to “Default” still appear for users set to other language than the default?
  3. Do you configure your learning items and content objects with specific languages (e.g., EN, EN_US, EN_GB), or do you default to “Default” for broader accessibility? What pros/cons have you experienced with each approach?
  4. Has your organization implemented any workarounds or best practices to ensure item visibility across multiple language settings, especially when the same content is relevant globally?
  5. Is there any known limitation where localized language settings (EN_GB or EN_US) fail to inherit or display items configured with "Default" language in catalogs, search, or assignments?
  6. Do you localize item metadata (title, description, language) to match user locale settings? If so, how do you manage consistency across language variants?

entry.title: All Datasphere Functions in One File
entry.content:
All Datasphere Functions in One File – A Handy Resource for Consultants

Hi SAP Community! 👋

I wanted to share something I’ve recently put together that might save you some time and effort, especially if you're working with SAP Datasphere and don't have access to Joule for Consultants.

During my work with graphical views, I realized how helpful it would be to have all the available functions in one place. So I created a File that contains the complete list of functions you can use in a graphical view transformation.

The file includes three simple columns:

  • ID – A running number

  • Function Title – The name of the function

  • Text – An example of how to use it

You can use this file in several ways:

  • As a quick reference for transformations

  • To train a local GPT model or use it offline

  • To upload it to ChatGPT
    (If your organization allows access to the public version) and ask questions like “How to obtain this value 20290506 when I have a May 6th, 2025 date column?

💡 If you prefer not to upload content to an external tool, you can still explore it directly via the link below.

Note. To have it in Excel. Open Excel and drag and drop this .txt file there. Excel will separate the values in columns. 

🔧 And if you do want to use Joule for Consultants but haven’t set it up yet, here’s the official SAP guide to get started:
👉 How to Set Up Joule For Consultants

Hope this resource helps you in your daily work with Datasphere. If you find it useful, feel free to leave a comment or share it with your colleagues!

entry.title: Re: Example of a HANA Hierarchy Query for Incentive Manag...
entry.content:
Hi Ron

There are a couple of ways you could do it.  Some recursive SQL in a stored proc that would loop through the hierarchy level by level. This may be useful if you're doing this in a Stored proc.  Or try the built in Hierarchy functions of HANA.

They key is to loop through using RULEELEMENTOWNERSEQ and MANAGERSEQ in the CS_POSITION table (obviously taking care to effective date as well)

e.g if I wanted to see the people below the manager

(Note this query is NOT complete it's just a quick example to show the hierarchy function.  You'll need to join to any other tables you need)

SELECT
HIERARCHY_DISTANCE,
NAME,
PARENT_ID AS MANAGERSEQ
from HIERARCHY_DESCENDANTS (
SOURCE HIERARCHY (
source (SELECT
POS.NAME AS NAME,
POS.RULEELEMENTOWNERSEQ AS NODE_ID,
POS.MANAGERSEQ AS PARENT_ID
FROM CS_POSITION POS
JOIN CS_PERIOD PER
ON (
(
POS.effectivestartdate < per.enddate
AND POS.effectiveenddate >= per.enddate
)
OR (
POS.effectiveenddate > per.startdate
AND POS.effectiveenddate <= per.enddate
AND POS.islast = 1
)
)
AND PER.NAME = 'June 2025'
JOIN CS_CALENDAR CAL
ON CAL.NAME = 'Main Monthly Calendar'
AND CAL.REMOVEDATE = TO_DATE('01/01/2200','DD/MM/YYYY')
AND CAL.CALENDARSEQ = PER.CALENDARSEQ
WHERE PER.removedate = TO_DATE('01/01/2200','DD/MM/YYYY')
AND POS.removedate = TO_DATE('01/01/2200','DD/MM/YYYY')
) ORPHAN ROOT
)
START WHERE NAME = '001111_Gray_J_RSG'
) ORDER BY HIERARCHY_DISTANCE

 

 

Hopefully that gives some clues,


Regards

 

Phil.

References:

entry.title: Re: How use IPS for SAP AFC Roles
entry.content:
Hello Thorsten,

we plan to provide support for AFC Roles via SCIM and IPS. Idea is, that AFC Roles are synched from AFC via IPS to IAS, then they appear as IAS Groups. User Group assignments can be maintained in IAS and then those assignments are synced back from IAS via IPS to AFC. Only role assignments are synched, the role maintenance itself stays in AFC, and therefore the role read-only from IAS/IPS/SCIM perspective.

Please stay tuned and see the What's New page of SAP Advanced Financial Closing, when this feature will be available.

Thanks.

Best regards, 

Oliver

entry.title: Length of Product ID (material number) and old material number
entry.content:
Since S/4HANA runs in compatibility mode, both fields material number (product number, MARA-MATNR) and Old Material Number/Old Product Number (MARA-BISMT) have length of 18 char. More information on compatibility mode you will find in https://me.sap.com/notes/2269324.

If you enter a value longer than 18 char, an error message will apply.

Customers can request extension up to 40 char via expert configuration. This functionality is called Material Field Length Extension (MFLE).

Both fields (material number, old material number) are enabled to 40 char by MFLE expert configuration. There is no option to switch on one but not the other.

After the activation you can use SSCUI 102320 (Define Output Format of Material Numbers) to set the needed length of material number.

If you only want to extend the field Old Material Number to 40 char, you have to request MFLE activation. Once it is active, you can maintain settings in SSCUI 102320 (former transaction OMSL) where you can set the length of the material back to 18 char. The old material number length is then possible up to 40 characters.

Once MFLE is activated, it cannot be deactivated later.

Contact SAP to request a length extension for product numbers.

For more information on Product Numbers, see Product Numbers

What’s New: Extend Product Number Length | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Team-Based Notifications for different Task Type Assignments
entry.content:
What’s New?

Until now, notifications in SAP Cloud ALM were primarily driven by direct task assignments to individual users. With this new enhancement, when a team is assigned to a specific task type, a notification is automatically sent to all members of that team. This eliminates the need for assigning tasks individually while ensuring all relevant team members stay informed and engaged.

 

How it works? 

When a project lead/member assigns a team to a task , the system now sends a notification to each member of that team. Users receiving the notification can directly navigate to the associated task in context, making it easier to start collaborating or taking action immediately. This feature supports a more scalable and streamlined way of task delegation, especially in projects with large or cross-functional teams.

 

Real-World Example : 

Let’s say your project has two teams:

  • Team A: responsible for Testing tasks

  • Team B: responsible for Development tasks

Scenario:

  • A Testing task(project task) is created in the system.

  • The project manager assigns Team A to this task.

  • Result: All members of Team A receive a notification, and each of them can click on the notification to jump straight into the Testing task and begin their work.

If a user belongs to Team B only, they will not receive any notification, since their team was not assigned to the Testing task . This ensures targeted communication and minimizes notification noise.

 

Supported Task Types

This new functionality is available across most major task types in SAP Cloud ALM. Specifically, team-based notifications on task type assignment currently support:

  • Roadmap Tasks

  • User Stories

  • Requirements

  • Project Tasks

  • Sub-tasks

  • Defects

Exceptions:
This feature is not currently supported for: Quality Gates , Checklists

 

New Notification Types Introduced

To support this team-based assignment model, SAP Cloud ALM now includes dedicated notification types that trigger when your team is assigned to certain task types. The following new notification types are available: 

  • Tasks: assigned to your team

  • Requirements: assigned to your team

  • Defects: assigned to your team

These ensure that notifications are not only timely but also clearly categorized by content type, so users immediately understand what they’re being asked to engage with.

 

Additional Safeguards in Place : What Happens When Changing the Assigned Team?

As part of this enhancement, we've also introduced a built-in safeguard to ensure that changes in team assignments are not inconsistent if there is already a Responsible member assigned to the Task. Here’s how we handle this scenario:

Scenario
Let’s say:

  • Your project has multiple teams

  • Different users are assigned to different teams

  • You have a task assigned to User A

  • User A is not part of Team X

Now, you decide to change the team assignment of the task to Team X.

 What happens next?

  • The system will warn you that User A will be removed as the responsible person for the task, since they are not part of the newly assigned team.

  • You will be given a choice, based on effort and context:

    • If feasible, you can choose to keep User A assigned to the task.

    • Alternatively, if keeping them is not supported in that context, you can simply cancel the team change to avoid losing the individual assignment.

 

Summary : Why This Matters

  • Efficiency: Assigning at the team level reduces manual overhead and ensures consistency across similar tasks.

  • Clarity: Only relevant users receive notifications, which avoids confusion and overload.

  • Contextual Awareness: Users can navigate directly to the task type context, without hunting through the system.

entry.title: Season 2 Recap SAP HANA Cloud Videocast: Get an introduction to top of mind topics
entry.content:
Welcome to your one-stop-shop for all episodes of the second season of the SAP HANA Cloud Videocast.

This season, we’ve brought you eight episodes packed with insights, innovations, and expert discussions. Whether you’re just starting your journey with SAP HANA Cloud or looking to deepen your expertise, this blogpost will guide you through the recent season. Dive into it and stay informed in terms of SAP HANA Cloud.

E1: Native Multitenancy in SAP HANA Cloud

Video Link: Watch Now 

Discover the game-changing feature of native multitenancy in SAP HANA Cloud. SAP HANA Cloud expert Ruediger Karl and Product Manager Susen Poppe explore the concept, its benefits, and real-world use cases. Learn how this feature can revolutionize your architecture and how start leveraging its power today.

E2: SQL on Files in SAP HANA Cloud

Video Link: Watch Now 

Join our product expert Seungjoon Lee to uncover the potential of "SQL on Files" in SAP HANA Cloud. This feature enhances data management by enabling direct SQL queries on files in object storage, boosting flexibility and cost-efficiency. Find out how to use this innovation with SAP HANA Cloud and accelerate your analytical scenarios.

E3: Next Level Administration with SAP HANA Cloud's Gen AI Virtual Assistant

Video Link: Watch Now 

Step into the future of database administration with SAP HANA Cloud's new virtual assistant, powered by SAP Joule. Product expert José Ramos demonstrates how GenAI streamlines operational tasks, offers guided assistance, and provides quick status analysis. Explore how this enhancement boosts productivity and enhances user experience.

E4: Private IP Endpoint Support in SAP HANA Cloud

Video Link: Watch Now 

Join product expert Robert Waywell to discover how private IP endpoint support in SAP HANA Cloud reduces network exposure and ensures secure connections. Learn about integrating native hyperscaler services like AWS PrivateLink, GCP Private Service Connect, and Azure Private Link, and why this is crucial for your cloud strategy.

E5: SAP HANA Cloud’s Knowledge Graph Engine

Video Link: Watch Now 

Explore the power of the Knowledge Graph Engine in SAP HANA Cloud with product expert Shabana Samsudheen. Learn how it connects data with context and semantics, enabling smarter, faster decisions. Get insights into real-world use cases, such as navigating legal and tax regulations globally, and start your journey with hands-on exercises, blogs, and sessions.

E6: Modernize with SAP HANA Cloud

Video Link: Watch Now 

Discover the seamless path to migrating to SAP HANA Cloud with product expert Roman Belosludtsev. Learn about the cloud-native benefits, flexible consumption models, and future-proof technologies that SAP HANA Cloud offers. Explore tools and resources to get started and transform your database strategy today.

E7: Dipping into SAP HANA Cloud, data lake

Video Link: Watch Now 

Dive into the power of SAP HANA Cloud, data lake with product expert Jason Hinsperger. Learn why enterprises need a data lake and how SAP HANA Cloud simplifies its creation. With seamless integration and comprehensive capabilities, SAP HANA Cloud offers a unique experience for managing and leveraging data. Get inspired by real-world customer examples and start your data journey today.

E8: Cloud Meets On-Prem: Unlock Hybrid Scenarios with SAP HANA Cloud

Video Link: Watch Now: 

Join product expert Tae-Suk Son as he explores hybrid scenarios in SAP HANA Cloud. Discover how to extend on-prem applications, access innovative features like machine learning, and combine data from various sources. Explore use cases, including analytical workloads and clean core applications, and start your journey to the cloud today.

It's been an incredible season filled with innovation insights and practical tips and tricks. We hope these videos inspire and motivate you to start or continue leveraging the potential of SAP HANA Cloud for your use case.

Stay Connected, Stay Inspired!

Susen on behalf SAP HANA Cloud Videocast Team

 

PS: BIG THANK YOU to all speakers!

entry.title: SAP Business Network for Logistics 2506 – What’s New?
entry.content:
We’re excited to announce that the latest SAP Business Network for Logistics release, 2506, went live over the last weekend! This release brings enhancements across SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and Global Track and Trace.

Let’s explore the new features and updates.

 

Cross SAP Business Network for Logistics

SAP BTP Neo has been deprecated

SAP BTP, Neo environment has been deprecated and will be sunset on December 31st, 2025. This applies to SAP Business Network for Logistics. The official support for the current integration platform running on Neo environment will stop on July 18th, 2025.

Please switch to the new integration platform on SAP BTP, Multi-Cloud environment!

A respective SAP blog post is available: Announcement: New integration platform in SAP Business Network for Logistics

Migration Guides are available:

  • Integration Platform Migration Guide for Shippers
  • Integration Platform Migration Guide for Carriers
  • Integration Platform Migration Guide for Solution Owners
  • Integration Platform Migration Guide for Data Contributors

In the ‘Message Integration Log’ for both carriers and shippers, a notification is displayed in case the account is still on Neo.

Note: Data federation capabilities are supported on Multi-Cloud only.

Availability of Ukrainian language

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration and SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace now support the Ukrainian language, ensuring inclusivity for carriers and shippers operating in Ukrainian-speaking regions.

 

SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration

Updated How-To Guide

The How-to-Guide for Accessing SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration from External Systems has been updated.

Email Notification Harmonization

To improve readability and provide consistent communication for freight collaboration, the footer section of email notifications has been harmonized across both the shipper and carrier user groups.

Contracting: 'Consumer API for Freight Booking': Removal of Fields with NULL Values

To improve data quality and API efficiency, fields with NULL values are no longer sent in the payload of the 'Consumer API for Freight Booking'.

Dock Appointment Scheduling: New Fields Available in the ‘Self-Book Dock Appointments’ app

Carriers can now use additional fields—‘Planned Start Date and Time’, ‘Planned End Date and Time’, and ‘Overbooking’ (indicates Enabled/Disabled)—while booking appointments.


 

Dock Appointment Scheduling: Harmonized Values in the ‘Self-Book Dock Appointments’ App

The ‘Self-Book Dock Appointments’ app now offers consistent values (such as for columns, sort, filter, and group) across the tabs ‘Ready for Booking’ and ‘Booked Appointments’.

New Filter for ‘Manage Dock Appointments' App

Shippers can now apply a new optional ‘Loading Point’ filter in the ‘Manage Dock Appointments’ app’s table view. The ability to save and reuse filtered views simplifies business workflows.

 

SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace

Link to Sample Codes for Template Apps Removed

The link to sample codes for template apps has been removed from the product page on SAP Help Portal.

Email Rules Now Support New Operators

To improve rule-building flexibility, three new operators —‘Starts with’, ‘Ends with’, and ‘Contains’—are now available for conditional email notifications in the ‘Manage Models’ app.


Updates to System Connections

Solution owners configuring document links can now define connections where the System ID and Connection ID differ, offering greater system design flexibility.

‘Manage Event Logs’ App: Export to Spreadsheet Enhancements

Starting June 2025, exporting worklist data for solution owners will require specific SAP authorization roles. Without these roles assigned, users will not see the export option in the UI.

 

In addition, the number of potential dataset exports has been limited to 2,000 records.

 

All Fulfillment Tracking apps: Export to Spreadsheet Enhancements

Please refer to the above paragraph on ‘Manage Event Logs’.

‘Track Shipments’ app: Enhancement to ‘Information’ Tab

A new ‘Transportation Mode’ field has been added to the 'Shipment Details' pages. Further, there is enhanced logic for the ‘Carrier’ field. Data display is prioritized as follows:

  • Carrier name from the CarrierDescription code list, followed by
  • Carrier name in the ‘Manage Business Profile’ app, then
  • Carrier identifier when names are unavailable.

 

 

We hope you find these updates valuable in optimizing your logistics and supply chain operations. Have feedback or questions? Let us know in the comments below—we’d love to hear from you!

 ---------------------

The above summarizes the key innovations for 2506. We hope you enjoy the new capabilities. Thanks for reading the blog post and stay tuned! 

A full overview of the 2506 release can be found under the following link.

To learn more about SAP Business Network for Logistics visit SAP - Business Network for Logistics.

For further questions and feedback related to the blog, please check the Q&A area and feel free to post your questions in the link with the tag SAP Business Network for Logistics.
